title: Your Microbiome: The Other 99% of Your DNA

contentBlocks.text: You are mostly not you. Your body contains roughly 37 trillion human cells, but over 100 trillion bacterial cells. For every human gene you carry, there are 100 microbial genes. This vast ecosystem of microorganisms - your microbiome - influences everything from digestion to mood to immune function. Scientists are discovering we're not individuals but ecosystems, and our health depends on keeping our microbial partners happy.

The gut microbiome is the most studied and influential. These trillions of bacteria in your intestines break down food you can't digest, produce vitamins, train your immune system, and manufacture neurotransmitters. They're not passive passengers - they're active participants in your biology, so integrated that some scientists question where 'you' end and 'them' begin.

Your gut bacteria produce about 95% of your body's serotonin, the neurotransmitter associated with happiness and well-being. They manufacture GABA, which reduces anxiety. They produce short-chain fatty acids that affect brain function. There's a 'gut-brain axis' - a two-way communication highway between your intestines and your brain, mediated by microbes.

Studies show that transplanting gut bacteria from anxious mice into calm mice makes the calm mice anxious. Transplanting bacteria from obese humans into mice makes the mice gain weight. Depression, anxiety, autism, and Parkinson's disease all show altered microbiome patterns. Correlation isn't causation, but the evidence suggests microbes influence mental health significantly.

Antibiotics are life-saving but devastating to the microbiome. A single course can reduce microbial diversity for months or years. Some species may never return. This matters because diversity appears protective - people with less diverse microbiomes have higher rates of obesity, diabetes, inflammatory diseases, and allergies. Modern hygiene may have made us too clean for our own good.

The 'hygiene hypothesis' proposes that reduced childhood exposure to microbes contributes to rising allergy and autoimmune disease rates. Kids who grow up on farms with animals have lower allergy rates. Children delivered by C-section, who miss exposure to the birth canal's microbes, show higher rates of asthma and allergies. Early microbial exposure trains the immune system.

Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) - yes, transplanting stool from healthy donors - has proven remarkably effective for recurrent C. difficile infections, with cure rates over 90%. Researchers are now testing FMT for obesity, depression, autism, and inflammatory bowel disease. The idea of 'poop transplants' sounds absurd but represents sophisticated ecosystem engineering.

Your diet directly shapes your microbiome within days. Fiber feeds beneficial bacteria that produce anti-inflammatory compounds. High-fat, low-fiber diets favor different species associated with inflammation. This creates a feedback loop: good bacteria produce compounds that make you crave healthy foods; bad bacteria make you crave junk food. Your microbes influence your behavior to serve their interests.

Everyone's microbiome is unique, like a fingerprint. Identical twins share only about 50% of bacterial species despite shared genetics and early environment. Your microbiome is shaped by birth method, diet, medications, geography, pets, and thousands of other factors. This uniqueness might explain why identical diets or treatments work differently for different people.

The microbiome revolution is rewriting our understanding of health. We're not autonomous individuals but symbiotic ecosystems. Diseases once considered purely genetic or environmental involve microbial components. Treatments might increasingly focus on cultivating healthy microbial communities rather than just targeting human cells. The future of medicine may involve less killing bacteria and more learning to garden them. We're finally recognizing that we've been ecosystems all along - we just forgot to count most of our members.

quiz.question: Approximately how many bacterial cells does your body contain compared to human cells?
options: Nearly three times as many bacterial cells
quiz.explanation: Your body contains roughly 37 trillion human cells but over 100 trillion bacterial cells - nearly three times as many microbes as human cells.

quiz.question: What percentage of your body's serotonin is produced by gut bacteria?
quiz.explanation: Gut bacteria produce about 95% of your body's serotonin, the neurotransmitter associated with happiness and well-being.

quiz.question: What is FMT and what has it proven effective for?
options: Fecal microbiota transplantation for C. difficile infections
quiz.explanation: FMT (fecal microbiota transplantation) involves transplanting stool from healthy donors and has proven remarkably effective for recurrent C. difficile infections with over 90% cure rates.

quiz.question: What does the 'hygiene hypothesis' propose?
options: Reduced childhood microbial exposure contributes to rising allergy rates
quiz.explanation: The hygiene hypothesis proposes that reduced childhood exposure to microbes contributes to rising rates of allergies and autoimmune diseases by under-training the immune system.

quiz.question: How quickly can your diet change your microbiome?
quiz.explanation: Your diet can directly shape your microbiome within days, with different foods promoting different bacterial species.
